task: I need to put together a simple handoff note for a customer issue that’s been bouncing around a bit, and I want it to be easy for the next person to follow. What’s the cleanest way to lay out the status, what’s already been checked, and what still needs attention without making it too wordy?

turns.agent_response:
Use a short, fixed format so the next person can scan it fast.

### Simple handoff note format
**1. Issue / case #**  
**2. Current status**  
**3. What’s already been checked**  
**4. What’s confirmed**  
**5. What still needs attention**  
**6. Next step / owner**  
**7. Time last updated**

### Example
**Case:** 12345  
**Status:** Waiting on carrier reply  
**Checked:** Order record, tracking, customer email, internal notes  
**Confirmed:** Shipment left warehouse on 4/26; delay started after carrier pickup  
**Needs attention:** Carrier response; confirm delivery estimate  
**Next step / owner:** Follow up with carrier and update customer — Alex  
**Last updated:** 4/29, 10:15 AM

### A few rules to keep it clean
- Keep **confirmed** separate from **pending**
- Use **facts only**; don’t guess
- List checks in the order you did them
- Put the **next action** in one clear line

If you want, I can turn this into a **copy-paste template** with blank fields.

user_simulator.text:
Yeah, that’s basically the right shape. Clean and easy to scan.

I’d keep it even tighter, though, like this:

**Case / customer:**  
**Current status:**  
**Checked so far:**  
**Confirmed facts:**  
**Still needed:**  
**Next step / owner:**  
**Last updated:**  

That keeps the “checked” and “confirmed” parts separate, which matters. I’ve seen notes get muddy when people mix what they looked at with what they actually proved.

If you want it to work well for handoff, I’d also make sure:
- each line is one sentence or a short phrase
- no filler like “just following up” or “working on it”
- if something is waiting on someone else, say who

Example:

**Case / customer:** 45821 / J. Smith  
**Current status:** Waiting on carrier update  
**Checked so far:** Order record, tracking, warehouse notes, customer emails  
**Confirmed facts:** Shipment left on 4/26; delay started after pickup  
**Still needed:** Carrier ETA and reason for delay  
**Next step / owner:** Follow up with carrier — Maria  
**Last updated:** 4/29, 10:15 AM

That’s the kind of note somebody can pick up without having to dig. If you want, I can also make it into a super short template for email or CRM notes.

turns.agent_response:
That’s a good structure. I’d keep it exactly that tight and use the same fields every time.

### Clean handoff note template
**Case / customer:**  
**Current status:**  
**Checked so far:**  
**Confirmed facts:**  
**Still needed:**  
**Next step / owner:**  
**Last updated:**  

### A couple quick rules
- Keep **Checked so far** separate from **Confirmed facts**
- Use short phrases, not paragraphs
- If something is waiting on someone else, name who
- Stick to facts only; don’t mix in guesses

### Example
**Case / customer:** 45821 / J. Smith  
**Current status:** Waiting on carrier update  
**Checked so far:** Order record, tracking, warehouse notes, customer emails  
**Confirmed facts:** Shipment left on 4/26; delay started after pickup  
**Still needed:** Carrier ETA and reason for delay  
**Next step / owner:** Follow up with carrier — Maria  
**Last updated:** 4/29, 10:15 AM  

If you want, I can also turn this into a **one-line-per-case table** for faster scanning.

user_simulator.text:
Yeah, that works. Tight enough, and the same fields every time is the part that matters.

I’d use that as the standard and not dress it up. The split between **Checked so far** and **Confirmed facts** is the right call — keeps people from blurring what they reviewed with what they actually know.

The one-line-per-case table could be useful too, but this template is solid as-is for a handoff note.
